card.title: Why 'Junk DNA' Isn't Junk
card.teaser: 98% of your DNA doesn't code for proteins. For decades scientists called it junk. They were wrong.
card.coverImagePrompt: A luminous double helix unraveling against deep navy space, most strands glowing gold instead of the usual blue, tiny regulatory switches visible at intervals, cinematic microscopic detail

lessons.title: The 98% problem
lessons.body: When scientists finished sequencing the human genome in 2003, they found something strange: only about 2% of our DNA codes for proteins — the classical definition of a 'gene'. The other 98% was written off as evolutionary leftovers. For decades, textbooks called it 'junk DNA'. Then biologists took a closer look.

visual.imagePrompt: A golden DNA strand being pulled out of a trash bin against a clean white lab background, dramatic lighting, metaphorical

lessons.title: What's actually in there
lessons.body: That so-called junk is a sprawling control room. It contains promoters that switch genes on, enhancers that crank them louder, insulators that block neighboring genes from interfering, and long non-coding RNAs that shuttle signals around the cell. There are also viral relics called transposons — bits of ancient infections stitched permanently into your chromosomes.

keyTerms.definition: DNA that doesn't directly produce a protein but often has regulatory roles
A short sequence that boosts the expression of a distant gene
A 'jumping gene' that can copy itself across the genome; many come from ancient viruses

visual.imagePrompt: A sci-fi control panel with glowing sliders and dials, each labeled as a gene, with a DNA strand threading through the background

lessons.title: Regulation is where the action is
lessons.body: Every cell in your body has the same DNA — but a liver cell and a neuron look and act nothing alike. The difference isn't which genes they have; it's which genes are turned on, when, and how loudly. Most of that choreography is directed by non-coding DNA. Roughly 80% of the genome shows signs of being biochemically active, according to the ENCODE project.

visual.imagePrompt: A conductor's baton raised over an orchestra pit, where each musician is a glowing cell, sheet music spelled out in DNA letters

lessons.title: When the junk goes wrong
lessons.body: Mutations in non-coding regions are linked to schizophrenia, autoimmune disease, heart conditions, and dozens of cancers. In 2013, researchers traced a stubborn type of melanoma to a single mutation sitting in the promoter of a gene — a spot that would have been dismissed as junk in 1995. The mutation didn't change any protein. It just turned the volume up.
lessons.funFact: About 8% of your genome is old viral DNA. Some of it has been repurposed — one viral gene is now essential for building the placenta that kept you alive before birth.

visual.imagePrompt: A single red-highlighted letter in a long blue strand of DNA code, magnifying glass zooming in, clinical and precise

lessons.title: A vocabulary problem
lessons.body: Calling something 'junk' tells you more about the namer than the thing named. The genome isn't mostly garbage with a little software — it's mostly software, with a little hardware. Biologists now mostly avoid the term 'junk DNA' altogether. It turns out naming something useless is a great way to stop looking at it.

quiz.question: Roughly what percentage of human DNA directly codes for proteins?
quiz.explanation: Protein-coding sequences make up only about 1-2% of the genome. The rest is mostly regulatory and structural DNA.

quiz.question: What does an enhancer do?
options: Boosts the expression of a gene, sometimes far away on the chromosome
quiz.explanation: Enhancers are short regulatory sequences that turn up the volume on distant genes.

quiz.question: Why do different cell types look so different despite identical DNA?
options: They express different subsets of the same genes, largely via non-coding DNA
They gain new DNA as they develop
Mutations accumulate uniquely in each cell type
quiz.explanation: The genome is the same; the regulatory program reading it is what differs. That program is mostly written in non-coding DNA.

sources.title: An integrated encyclopedia of DNA elements in the human genome
Highly Recurrent TERT Promoter Mutations in Human Melanoma
